claim: POST /api/auth/reset-password never deletes the consumed token from password_reset_tokens. The SELECT only fetches user_id and expires_at, so resetData.id is undefined; the DELETE then filters by .eq('id', resetData.user_id) which compares the user_id UUID against the tokens.id column — never matches. Stale tokens accumulate in the DB indefinitely.
evidence: server/src/routes/auth.ts:566-571: const { data: resetData, error: resetError } = await supabase.from('password_reset_tokens').select('user_id, expires_at').eq('token', token).single(); ... server/src/routes/auth.ts:597-598: await supabase.from('password_reset_tokens').delete().eq('id', resetData.user_id)
runtimeTrigger: Call POST /api/auth/reset-password with a valid (token, newPassword) — password is updated, response is 200, but the token row persists. Repeat with the same token after the 1h expiry — PostgREST returns 0 rows affected (filter does not match), so the token row is also not pruned by expiry-sweep logic if any exists.

claim: Translated strings from Google Translate are interpolated unescaped into HTML email bodies. tx() returns the raw translatedText; the htmlBody template literal embeds it via ${...} with no HTML escaping. If the translation contains HTML-like tokens (e.g., for languages with rich punctuation, or in a cache-poisoning scenario), they could break email layout. Most modern email clients strip <script>/on* but allow CSS injection.
evidence:
server/src/lib/email.ts:160-161:

${heading}

${intro}

; tx() at lines 29-58 returns the raw translatedText without sanitization; same pattern at lines 215-216 (reset) and 278, 281, 284, 289, 293 (reminder).
runtimeTrigger: Send any localized email to a target — the translated strings land in the HTML body verbatim. With a malicious or compromised translation cache entry, a translation of 'Welcome' returning '' would render (though most clients neutralize the handler).

claim: ACCESS_TOKEN_SECRET falls back to a hardcoded literal 'dev-secret-change-in-production' if JWT_SECRET is not set. If an operator deploys without setting JWT_SECRET, all JWTs are signed with a publicly-known string and any attacker can forge access tokens for any userId. The string itself even reads as a TODO.
runtimeTrigger: Deploy server without JWT_SECRET env var. Sign a JWT with payload {userId: '<any uuid>', email: 'attacker@x'} using HS256 and the literal 'dev-secret-change-in-production' as the secret. Send Authorization: Bearer <forged>. Middleware accepts it, looks up the user, sets req.userId. Full auth bypass.

claim: PUT /api/users/profile accepts preferred_language as an arbitrary string with no validation against SUPPORTED_EMAIL_LANGS or any other enum. An attacker can store any value (e.g., ../../etc/passwd, <script>alert(1)</script>). The value is used to call Google Translate with target=<lang>; Google will reject invalid codes, but the value also gets read by the cron jobs and could appear in PostHog events or logs.
evidence: server/src/routes/users.ts:81: const preferred_language = body.preferred_language ?? body.preferredLanguage ?? null; ... no validation before upsert at line 122-132.
runtimeTrigger: PUT /api/users/profile with body {"preferredLanguage": "../../etc/passwd"} — value is stored in user_profiles. Subsequent cron job reads the value, attempts to call Google Translate (fails silently), and may log the raw value.

claim: requireAuth attaches a Supabase client built with the SERVICE ROLE key to every authenticated request. The codebase currently scopes all queries by req.userId, so the practical risk is low, but any future route handler that uses req.supabase without an explicit user_id filter would bypass RLS and read/write any user's data. This is a defense-in-depth gap (no second line of defense).
evidence: server/src/middleware/auth.ts:173-178: req.supabase = createClient(runtimeSupabaseUrl || 'https://placeholder.supabase.co', runtimeSupabaseKey || 'placeholder', { auth: { autoRefreshToken: false, persistSession: false } })
runtimeTrigger: Future code path: any new route handler that calls req.supabase.from('users').select('*') without .eq('id', req.userId) returns ALL users. No RLS enforcement.

claim: POST /api/auth/refresh hardcodes the new refresh token's TTL to 30 days, ignoring the original login's rememberMe flag. A user who logged in with rememberMe=false (7 days) and then refreshes gets a 30-day refresh token they originally declined.
runtimeTrigger: User logs in with rememberMe=false (7-day TTL). After accessToken expires (15 min), client calls /api/auth/refresh. New refreshToken is created with 30-day TTL. User's session is now 4x longer-lived than what they consented to.
